question: Build a counter app using Provider and ChangeNotifier.
answer: Create a ChangeNotifier model that holds the count and calls notifyListeners() on change. Expose it via ChangeNotifierProvider at the top of the tree, and read/update it in widgets with context.watch<CounterModel>() (rebuilds) or context.read<CounterModel>() (fire-and-forget calls).
tip: Use context.read inside onPressed handlers and context.watch inside build—mixing them up is a common interview slip.

question: Build a simple todo list with add, toggle-complete, and delete.
answer: Keep a List<Todo> in a StatefulWidget. Adding/removing/toggling all call setState to mutate the list and trigger a rebuild. Render with ListView.builder and use each item's id as a ValueKey so Flutter tracks identity correctly across reorders/deletes.
tip: Always give list items a stable key when the list can be reordered or filtered—prevents state 'jumping' between items.

question: Implement a debounced search field in Flutter.
answer: Listen to the TextEditingController, cancel any pending Timer on each keystroke, and start a new short-delay timer that fires the actual API call—so a network request only fires after the user pauses typing.
tip: This is the imperative Timer-based equivalent of RxJS's debounceTime—know both approaches (Timer vs rxdart's debounce).

question: Implement infinite scroll pagination in a ListView.
answer: Attach a ScrollController to the ListView and listen for the scroll position nearing maxScrollExtent; when close enough, fetch the next page and append it to the data list with setState. Guard with an isLoading flag to avoid firing duplicate requests.
tip: Always dispose the ScrollController and guard against calling setState after the widget is disposed (check 'mounted').

question: Fetch and render API data using FutureBuilder.
answer: Store the Future in initState (not in build, or it re-fetches on every rebuild), then pass it to FutureBuilder and branch on snapshot.connectionState and snapshot.hasError to render loading, error, or data states.
tip: Creating the Future inside build() is a classic bug—it restarts the network call on every rebuild.

question: Build a live countdown timer using Stream and StreamBuilder.
answer: Use Stream.periodic to emit a decreasing value every second, take() to limit it, and feed it to a StreamBuilder so the UI updates automatically each tick without manual setState calls.
tip: Stream.periodic starts emitting immediately on subscription—use .map or an initial value if you need to show the starting count first.

question: Build a login form with email/password validation.
answer: Wrap fields in a Form with a GlobalKey<FormState>. Each TextFormField validates its own input (email format, min password length); on submit, call formKey.currentState!.validate() and only proceed if it returns true.
tip: Use autovalidateMode: AutovalidateMode.onUserInteraction so errors appear as the user types, not only on submit.

question: Build a reusable custom button widget with a loading state.
answer: Wrap the design in a StatelessWidget exposing label, onPressed, and isLoading as parameters. When isLoading is true, disable the button (pass null as the callback) and swap the label for a small CircularProgressIndicator.
tip: Disabling by passing null to onPressed is the idiomatic Flutter way—no need for a separate 'enabled' flag.

question: Build a bottom navigation bar that preserves each tab's state.
answer: Use BottomNavigationBar to switch a selected index, but render all tab pages inside an IndexedStack instead of swapping widgets directly—IndexedStack keeps every child alive in the tree (just hidden), preserving scroll position and state when switching tabs.
tip: Swapping the body widget directly (e.g. with a switch statement) rebuilds each tab from scratch—IndexedStack is the fix.

question: Build a layout that switches between mobile and tablet designs.
answer: Use LayoutBuilder to read the available width from constraints.maxWidth and branch the widget tree at a breakpoint (e.g. 600px)—rendering a single column on narrow screens and a two-pane layout on wider ones.
tip: Prefer LayoutBuilder's constraints over MediaQuery's screen size when the widget could be nested inside something narrower than the full screen (e.g. a side panel).

question: Implement a counter using the Bloc/Cubit pattern.
answer: Define a Cubit<int> exposing increment()/decrement() methods that call emit() with the new state. Provide it with BlocProvider and rebuild the UI with BlocBuilder<CounterCubit, int>.
tip: Cubit state should be immutable—emit a brand-new value/object each time rather than mutating in place.

question: Add an auth-token interceptor with automatic refresh using Dio.
answer: Register an InterceptorsWrapper that attaches the current access token to every outgoing request in onRequest. In onError, check for a 401, call the refresh-token endpoint, update stored tokens, and retry the original request via handler.resolve(await dio.fetch(options)).
tip: Use a separate Dio instance (without the interceptor) for the refresh-token call itself to avoid infinite retry loops.

question: Write a Dart function to check if a string is a palindrome.
answer: Normalize the string (lowercase, strip non-alphanumeric characters if needed), then compare it to its reverse. This runs in O(n) time and O(n) space.
tip: Ask the interviewer whether to ignore case/spaces/punctuation before coding—shows you clarify requirements.

question: Write a Dart function to compute the nth Fibonacci number, and explain how to optimize the naive recursive version.
answer: The naive recursive solution recomputes overlapping subproblems, giving O(2^n) time. Adding memoization (caching already-computed results in a Map) brings it down to O(n) time and O(n) space. An iterative version achieves O(n) time with O(1) space.
tip: Mention Big-O for both the naive and optimized versions—interviewers want to see you recognize overlapping subproblems.

question: Write a Dart function to flatten an arbitrarily nested list.
answer: Recursively walk each element: if it's itself a List, recurse into it and spread the flattened result; otherwise add it directly to the output. This handles any depth of nesting.
tip: Dart's dynamic/List<dynamic> typing makes this natural—mention how you'd type it more strictly with generics if asked.

question: Implement a generic debounce function from scratch in Dart.
answer: A debounce wraps a callback so repeated calls within a delay window cancel the pending invocation and restart the timer—only the last call within the quiet period actually executes. Implemented with a closure holding a single Timer? across calls.
tip: This is the same idea behind fcoding-debounced-search but generalized as a reusable utility, not tied to a TextField.

question: Solve the Two Sum problem in Dart.
answer: Iterate through the list once, tracking each value's index in a Map. For each element, check whether target - element already exists in the map—if so, return both indices. This runs in O(n) time and O(n) space, versus O(n²) for the brute-force nested loop.
tip: Always mention the brute-force O(n²) approach first, then explain the hashmap trade-off (time for space)—shows structured thinking.

question: Build a stopwatch with start, pause, and reset.
answer: Use a Timer.periodic (e.g. every 30-50ms) to increment an elapsed-time counter and call setState. Start creates the timer, pause cancels it while keeping the elapsed value, and reset cancels the timer and zeroes the counter.
tip: Cancel the existing timer before creating a new one on 'start' to avoid accidentally running two timers at once if the button is tapped twice.

question: Build a quiz app that tracks the score across questions.
answer: Keep a List<Question>, a currentIndex, and a score in state. Selecting an answer checks it against the correct answer, increments the score if correct, then advances currentIndex; when the index reaches the list length, show a results screen.
tip: Disable answer buttons immediately after the first tap (e.g. via a 'answered' flag) to prevent double-scoring from a fast double-tap.

question: Build a shopping cart with add, remove, quantity adjustment, and a running total.
answer: Model the cart as a Map<Product, int> (product to quantity) inside a ChangeNotifier. Adding increments the quantity (or inserts at 1), removing decrements/removes at 0, and a getter computes the total by summing price * quantity across entries.
tip: Compute the total as a derived getter, not a stored field—storing it separately risks it going out of sync with the actual cart contents.

question: Build a weather app screen that fetches and displays current conditions.
answer: Fetch weather data in initState and store the Future; render with FutureBuilder, mapping the response to a model (temperature, condition, icon). Add a manual refresh button that re-assigns the Future and calls setState to re-trigger the FutureBuilder.
tip: Cache the last successful result so a failed refresh can still show stale data with an error banner, rather than blanking the whole screen.
code:
late Future<Weather> weatherFuture;

void refresh() => setState(() => weatherFuture = api.fetchWeather(city));

question: Build a basic chat screen UI (message list + input bar).
answer: Render messages with ListView.builder(reverse: true) so the list stays pinned to the newest message at the bottom, feeding it a reversed data list (or just inserting new messages at index 0). Pair it with a bottom input row (TextField + send IconButton) wrapped in a SafeArea so it isn't hidden by the keyboard or home indicator.
tip: Wrap the Scaffold body in resizeToAvoidBottomInset (default true) so the input bar rides up above the keyboard automatically.

question: Build an image gallery using GridView.builder.
answer: GridView.builder with a SliverGridDelegateWithFixedCrossAxisCount (or ...WithMaxCrossAxisExtent for responsive column counts) lazily builds only the visible thumbnails, using Image.network with a placeholder/error builder for each cell.
tip: Use WithMaxCrossAxisExtent instead of a fixed count when the gallery needs to adapt to different screen widths without a manual breakpoint check.

question: Build an expandable/collapsible FAQ list.
answer: ExpansionTile handles the expand/collapse animation and state internally—just give each one a title (the question) and a children list (the answer). For a custom look, track expanded state per item manually with an AnimatedCrossFade or AnimatedSize.

question: Build a drag-to-reorder list.
answer: ReorderableListView handles the drag gesture and animation automatically; you just implement onReorder(oldIndex, newIndex) to update your underlying list's order (adjusting the index since Flutter passes newIndex as if the item hadn't been removed yet). Each child needs a unique key.
tip: Remember the classic off-by-one fix: if newIndex > oldIndex, decrement newIndex by 1 before inserting.

question: Build a reusable star-rating widget.
answer: Render N icon widgets in a Row, filling stars up to the current rating value; wrap each in a GestureDetector that sets the rating to its index+1 on tap and calls a passed-in onChanged callback so the parent controls the actual state.
tip: Keep the widget stateless and controlled (rating + onChanged passed in) rather than stateful, so it's reusable in a form with validation.

question: Build a search bar that filters a local list as the user types.
answer: Keep the full source list and a filtered list in state. On each TextField change, filter the source list case-insensitively and call setState to update the displayed list—no debounce/API call needed since it's all in-memory.
tip: Only debounce this if the filter triggers something expensive (like re-rendering thousands of items)—for small local lists, filtering on every keystroke is fine.

question: Build a screen with a custom-styled TabBar and three tabs.
answer: Use a DefaultTabController (or a manual TabController with a SingleTickerProviderStateMixin) with a TabBar in the AppBar's bottom slot, and a matching TabBarView in the body holding one widget per tab in the same order.
tip: DefaultTabController is simpler when you don't need to listen to tab-change events; use a manual TabController when you need to react to index changes elsewhere.

question: Build a draggable bottom sheet that expands from partial to full height.
answer: DraggableScrollableSheet lets a bottom sheet be dragged between a min, initial, and max fractional size of the screen, with its own scroll controller so content scrolls once the sheet reaches max size instead of dragging further.

question: Build a splash screen that navigates to home after a delay.
answer: In initState, start a Future.delayed (or check an async auth-status call) and then call Navigator.pushReplacement so the splash screen isn't left in the back stack (preventing the user from navigating back to it).
tip: Always check 'if (!mounted) return;' before navigating after an await—the widget could be disposed if the user backgrounds the app during the delay.

question: Build an onboarding intro slider with page indicator dots.
answer: Use a PageView.builder for the swipeable slides and listen to its PageController to track the current page index, rendering a row of dots that highlight the active index. A 'Next'/'Get Started' button on the last page navigates away.
tip: Persist a 'hasSeenOnboarding' flag (shared_preferences) so the slider only shows once, not every app launch.

question: Build a light/dark theme switcher using Provider.
answer: A ChangeNotifier holds a ThemeMode field and a toggle method that calls notifyListeners(). MaterialApp reads it with context.watch and passes it to its themeMode parameter, alongside separate theme and darkTheme definitions.
tip: Persist the chosen mode with shared_preferences so the user's choice survives an app restart.

question: Build a reusable snackbar utility function for success/error messages.
answer: Wrap ScaffoldMessenger.of(context).showSnackBar in a static helper that takes a message and a type (success/error), picking a background color and icon accordingly—centralizing styling so every screen shows consistent feedback with one function call.
tip: Call ScaffoldMessenger.of(context).hideCurrentSnackBar() first if messages can fire in quick succession, so they don't queue up awkwardly.

question: Build a widget that shows a banner when the device loses internet connectivity.
answer: The connectivity_plus package exposes Connectivity().onConnectivityChanged as a Stream; feed it into a StreamBuilder at the app's root, and conditionally show a persistent banner ('No internet connection') when the result is ConnectivityResult.none.
tip: Connectivity state (WiFi connected) doesn't guarantee actual internet access—for a stricter check, ping a lightweight endpoint too.

question: Implement a biometric (fingerprint/Face ID) login gate using local_auth.
answer: The local_auth package's authenticate() method triggers the native biometric prompt and returns a bool; check canCheckBiometrics first to confirm the device supports it, and always provide a fallback (PIN/password) path for devices or users without biometrics enrolled.

question: Implement picking an image and uploading it to an API with progress tracking.
answer: Pick the file with image_picker, then build a FormData with MultipartFile.fromFile and send it via Dio's post, passing an onSendProgress callback to update a progress indicator as bytes are uploaded.

question: Build a video player screen with play/pause controls.
answer: Initialize a VideoPlayerController from a network/asset source in initState, await initialize(), then wrap the VideoPlayer widget with custom play/pause buttons calling controller.play()/pause(). Always dispose the controller.
tip: Show a loading spinner until controller.value.isInitialized is true—the video widget renders a blank/zero-size box before that.

question: Build a custom circular progress indicator that shows a percentage in the center.
answer: Stack a CircularProgressIndicator(value: progress) (or a CustomPainter drawing an arc with Canvas.drawArc for full styling control) with a centered Text('${(progress * 100).round()}%') using a Stack + Alignment.center.

question: Implement the core logic for a basic calculator app (digits, operators, equals).
answer: Keep the current input string, a pending operator, and a stored first operand in state. Digit taps append to the display string; an operator tap stores the current value and operator, clearing the display for the next number; equals applies the stored operator between the stored value and the current display value.
tip: Handle 'AC' (clear all) and consecutive-operator-taps (replace the pending operator instead of stacking) as your key edge cases when asked to extend this.

question: Build a currency converter that fetches live exchange rates.
answer: Fetch a rates map (base currency to rate) from an API once and cache it in state; conversion itself is then a pure local calculation (amount * rate) on every keystroke, avoiding an API call per digit typed. Refresh the rates periodically or via a manual refresh button.
tip: Round display output to 2 decimal places but keep full precision internally for the actual calculation—rounding too early compounds error on repeated conversions.
code: double convert(double amount, double rate) => amount * rate;

question: Build a notes app that persists notes locally using Hive.
answer: Register a Hive adapter for the Note model (or store as a Map for simplicity), open a Hive box at startup, and treat the box like a reactive local database—use ValueListenableBuilder on box.listenable() to automatically rebuild the UI whenever notes are added, edited, or deleted, without manual setState plumbing.
tip: Open the Hive box once in main() before runApp(), not inside a widget's initState, so it's ready before the first frame needs it.

question: Build a favorite/bookmark toggle that persists across app restarts.
answer: Store favorited item IDs as a Set<String> (serialized to a List<String> for shared_preferences, since it can't store sets directly). Toggling adds/removes the ID from the set, persists it, and calls notifyListeners() so every screen showing that item reflects the change immediately.
tip: Centralize favorite state in one ChangeNotifier/Provider rather than per-screen local state—otherwise the same item can show as favorited on one screen and not on another.

question: Build a multi-step form wizard (e.g. a 3-step signup flow).
answer: Use a PageView with NeverScrollableScrollPhysics (so users can't swipe past validation) driven by a PageController; each step has its own Form/GlobalKey<FormState>, and 'Next' only advances the page if that step's form validates. Collect all step data into one shared model object passed down to each step.
tip: Keep one GlobalKey per step, not one shared key—reusing a single FormState key across pages that are still in the widget tree (even if off-screen) causes validation conflicts.

question: Implement date selection using showDatePicker, and format the result.
answer: showDatePicker returns a Future<DateTime?> that resolves to null if the user cancels. Store the selected date in state and format it for display using the intl package's DateFormat rather than manually concatenating date fields.
tip: Always null-check the result—showDatePicker returns null on cancel, and forgetting that check is a very common crash.

question: Build a 6-digit OTP verification input with auto-focus advance.
answer: Use 6 individual single-character TextFields, each with its own FocusNode and TextEditingController; on each field's onChanged, if a digit was entered, request focus on the next field's FocusNode (or the previous one on backspace/empty), and concatenate all controllers' text to validate the full code.
tip: Dispose all 6 FocusNodes and controllers in dispose()—forgetting even one in a list of similar objects is an easy leak to introduce.

question: Implement Google Sign-In with Firebase Auth.
answer: Trigger the native Google sign-in flow via the google_sign_in package to get an auth token, then exchange it for a Firebase credential with GoogleAuthProvider.credential(...) and sign in to Firebase via FirebaseAuth.instance.signInWithCredential(...), giving you a unified Firebase user regardless of provider.

question: Build a list with section headers that stick to the top while scrolling.
answer: Use a CustomScrollView with one SliverPersistentHeader(pinned: true) per section (implementing a SliverPersistentHeaderDelegate for the section label) followed by a SliverList of that section's items—so headers pin to the top as their section scrolls past, then get pushed off by the next section's header.

question: Build a shimmer/skeleton loading placeholder while content loads.
answer: The shimmer package wraps a gray placeholder layout (boxes matching the shape of real content) in a Shimmer.fromColors widget that animates a moving gradient over it. Show the shimmer skeleton while FutureBuilder's connectionState is waiting, then swap to real content once data arrives.
tip: Match the skeleton's shapes/sizes closely to the real content's layout—this avoids a jarring layout shift once real data replaces the placeholder.

question: Build a reusable error/empty-state widget pattern for list screens.
answer: A small stateless StateView widget takes an icon, message, and optional retry callback/button—reused across every screen for 'no results', 'no internet', and 'something went wrong', so the same three states aren't reimplemented ad hoc on each screen.
tip: Always give the empty/error state a retry action when the cause is recoverable (network error)—a dead-end message with no way forward frustrates users.

question: Implement a retry mechanism with exponential backoff for a failing API call.
answer: Wrap the API call in a loop up to a max attempt count; on failure, wait an increasing delay (e.g. doubling each time, optionally with jitter) before retrying, and rethrow once the max attempts are exhausted. This avoids hammering a struggling server while still recovering from transient failures.
tip: Only retry on transient errors (timeouts, 5xx)—retrying a 4xx client error (like invalid credentials) just wastes time since the outcome won't change.

question: Design an offline-first data layer that syncs local changes when connectivity returns.
answer: A repository always reads/writes to local storage (Hive/sqflite) first for instant UI response, queuing an 'unsynced' flag on each changed record. A background sync routine—triggered on connectivity restore or app resume—pushes queued changes to the API, then marks them synced or handles conflicts (e.g. last-write-wins or a merge strategy).
tip: Mention conflict resolution explicitly (e.g. compare updatedAt timestamps)—interviewers probing this topic want to see you've thought past the happy path.

question: Build a real-time chat feature using WebSockets.
answer: Connect with WebSocketChannel.connect(uri); its .stream is a broadcast Stream of incoming messages fed directly into a StreamBuilder, and channel.sink.add(message) sends outgoing messages. Always close the channel (channel.sink.close()) in dispose to avoid a dangling connection.
tip: Implement a reconnect-with-backoff strategy for when the socket drops—raw WebSocket connections don't auto-reconnect.

question: Add a search icon to an AppBar that expands into a search field.
answer: Toggle a boolean isSearching state; when true, replace the AppBar's title with a TextField and swap the search icon for a close icon that resets the state and clears the query. For a full search experience, Flutter's built-in showSearch(context: context, delegate: MySearchDelegate()) handles this pattern with its own dedicated screen.

question: Add unread-count badges to a bottom navigation bar's icons.
answer: Wrap each tab icon in a Badge widget (Material 3's built-in Badge, or a Stack + Positioned circle for older versions), conditionally showing it only when that tab's unread count is greater than zero, sourced from a shared state/provider tracking notification counts per tab.

question: Build a side drawer navigation menu.
answer: Pass a Drawer widget to Scaffold(drawer: ...), containing a ListView of menu items (often with a DrawerHeader showing user info at top); each item's onTap should first call Navigator.pop(context) to close the drawer, then navigate to the target screen.
tip: Always pop the drawer before navigating—forgetting this leaves the drawer open behind the new screen's transition.

question: Build a settings screen with toggle switches and a slider.
answer: Each setting (notifications on/off, font size) is a controlled widget—Switch(value: ..., onChanged: ...) or Slider(value: ..., onChanged: ...)—backed by a settings model/provider that persists changes immediately to shared_preferences as the user interacts, rather than requiring a separate 'Save' button.

question: Build a custom-styled slider from scratch using GestureDetector.
answer: Use a GestureDetector's onHorizontalDragUpdate to read the drag's local position, clamp it to the track's width, and convert it to a 0-1 value which drives the thumb's position and the value reported to the parent—giving full control over the visual design that the built-in Slider doesn't allow.

question: Build a simple color picker grid.
answer: Render a Wrap or GridView of predefined color swatches, each a tappable circle that sets the selected color in state and shows a check/border on the currently selected one; expose the selection via an onColorSelected callback so the parent controls what happens with it.

question: Build a multi-select dropdown for choosing multiple tags.
answer: Show current selections as chips, and tapping opens a showModalBottomSheet or dialog with a checkbox list bound to a Set<String> of selected values; toggling a checkbox adds/removes from the set, and closing the sheet returns the updated set to the parent.

question: Build an autocomplete text field that suggests matches as the user types.
answer: Flutter's built-in Autocomplete<T> widget handles the overlay suggestion list, keyboard navigation, and selection callback for you—just provide optionsBuilder to filter your data source based on the current text value.

question: Format a phone number field as the user types (e.g. (123) 456-7890).
answer: Attach a custom TextInputFormatter to the TextField's inputFormatters that strips non-digit characters from the raw input, then re-inserts formatting characters (parentheses, dash) at fixed digit positions, returning a new TextEditingValue with the cursor repositioned correctly.
tip: Getting cursor position right after reformatting is the tricky part—test backspacing in the middle of the string, not just typing forward.

question: Write a reusable validator that chains multiple rules (required, min length, pattern).
answer: Compose small validator functions (String? Function(String?)) into one that runs each in order and returns the first non-null error message, short-circuiting on the first failure—so a single field can combine 'required' + 'min length' + 'format' rules declaratively.

question: Build a form where the user can dynamically add or remove fields (e.g. multiple phone numbers).
answer: Keep a List of controllers (one per dynamic field) in state. 'Add' appends a new TextEditingController and rebuilds; 'remove' disposes that specific controller and removes it from the list. Use the list index (or a generated id, not the controller itself) as each row's Key.
tip: Dispose every controller when removed individually, and dispose any remaining ones in the widget's own dispose()—leaking controllers here is easy since the list length changes.

question: Download a file with Dio and show download progress.
answer: dio.download(url, savePath, onReceiveProgress: (received, total)) streams the file directly to disk while reporting progress, avoiding loading the whole file into memory first—important for large files.
tip: Guard against total == -1 (some servers don't send Content-Length)—show an indeterminate spinner instead of dividing by -1.

question: Build a custom disk cache manager for downloaded images.
answer: On first request, check if a hashed version of the URL exists as a file in the app's cache directory (path_provider's getTemporaryDirectory()); if not, download it and write it to disk before returning the bytes, so subsequent requests read from disk instead of the network.
tip: In practice, prefer the well-tested cached_network_image/flutter_cache_manager packages over rolling your own—but knowing the underlying mechanism is exactly what this question tests.

question: Implement infinite-scroll pagination in a GridView.
answer: Same pattern as an infinite-scroll ListView: attach a ScrollController, detect nearing maxScrollExtent, and append the next page's items to the data list—the only difference is the grid uses a SliverGridDelegate instead of a linear layout, so the same scroll-position logic applies unchanged.

question: Build a list where items animate in and out when added/removed (AnimatedList).
answer: AnimatedList requires a GlobalKey<AnimatedListState>; instead of calling setState directly, call listKey.currentState!.insertItem(index) or removeItem(index, builder), which drives an animation for exactly that item while keeping the underlying data list in sync manually.
tip: removeItem's builder must render what the item looked like BEFORE removal (since it's animating out an item no longer in the data list)—a common source of bugs.

question: Implement a Hero animation from a grid thumbnail to a full-screen detail view.
answer: Wrap the thumbnail image and the detail screen's full image in matching Hero(tag: item.id, ...) widgets; when navigating between the two routes via Navigator.push, Flutter automatically animates the shared element between its two positions/sizes.

question: Implement a custom page transition animation (e.g. slide-up) using PageRouteBuilder.
answer: PageRouteBuilder lets you override transitionsBuilder, wrapping the destination page in an animated widget (like SlideTransition) driven by the provided animation controller—giving full control over enter/exit transitions beyond MaterialPageRoute's default.

question: Implement per-tab navigation stacks in a bottom-navigation app (each tab keeps its own back stack).
answer: Give each tab its own nested Navigator (with a unique GlobalKey<NavigatorState>) inside an IndexedStack, so pushing a detail screen within Tab A doesn't affect Tab B's stack, and switching tabs preserves each one's navigation history independently.
tip: Override the root PopScope/WillPopScope to pop the active tab's nested Navigator first, and only exit the app if that tab's stack is already at its root.

question: Handle an incoming deep link and navigate to the corresponding screen.
answer: Parse the incoming URI's path/query parameters into a route name and arguments, then use the navigator (ideally via go_router's declarative route matching, or a manual Navigator.pushNamed) to open the matching screen—handling the case where the app is cold-started from the link versus already running.
tip: Test both cold-start (app not running) and warm (app in background) deep link cases separately—they often take different code paths.

question: Implement an async counter/data-fetcher using Riverpod's AsyncNotifier.
answer: AsyncNotifier<T> exposes state as an AsyncValue<T> (data/loading/error) automatically, and its build() method is where you fetch the initial data. Methods can update state optimistically or re-fetch, and the UI just calls .when(data:, loading:, error:) on the watched state.

question: Implement a repository that returns cached data immediately and falls back to it if the network call fails.
answer: The repository first tries the remote data source; on success, it writes the fresh result to the local cache and returns it. On failure (timeout, no connection), it catches the error and returns whatever is in the local cache instead of propagating the failure, only throwing if the cache is also empty.

question: Combine debouncing with request cancellation for a search-as-you-type feature.
answer: Debounce keystrokes with a Timer as usual, but also cancel the previous in-flight request using a Dio CancelToken before firing a new one—this prevents a slow earlier response from arriving after a newer one and overwriting the UI with stale results (a race condition debounce alone doesn't fully solve).
tip: Debounce reduces how often you fire requests; CancelToken protects against the ones that DO fire out of order—both are needed for a fully correct implementation.

question: Implement a throttle function that limits how often an action can run.
answer: Unlike debounce (which waits for a pause), throttle guarantees the action runs at most once per fixed interval, executing immediately on the first call and then ignoring subsequent calls until the interval elapses—useful for things like limiting scroll-triggered API calls.
tip: Contrast clearly with debounce when asked: throttle guarantees regular execution during continuous activity; debounce guarantees execution only after activity stops.

question: Implement an LRU (Least Recently Used) cache in Dart.
answer: A LinkedHashMap preserves insertion order and lets you re-insert a key to move it to the end (marking it 'most recently used'). On get, remove and re-add the key to refresh its position; on put, evict the oldest entry (the map's first key) once capacity is exceeded. Both operations run in O(1).

question: Implement binary search on a sorted list in Dart.
answer: Binary search repeatedly halves the search range by comparing the middle element to the target, running in O(log n) time versus O(n) for a linear scan—but it requires the input list to already be sorted.
tip: State the precondition out loud: binary search only works on sorted data—an easy point interviewers check for.

question: Implement merge sort in Dart.
answer: Merge sort recursively splits the list in half until sublists have one element, then merges sorted sublists back together in order. It runs in guaranteed O(n log n) time (unlike quicksort's worst-case O(n²)) but uses O(n) extra space for the merge step.

question: Implement quicksort in Dart.
answer: Quicksort picks a pivot, partitions the list into elements less than and greater than the pivot, then recursively sorts each partition. Average time is O(n log n), but a poor pivot choice on already-sorted input can degrade to O(n²).
tip: Mention that choosing a random or median pivot mitigates the worst-case scenario on already-sorted or adversarial input.

question: Implement a singly linked list in Dart.
answer: A linked list is a chain of nodes, each holding a value and a reference to the next node, with a head pointer to the first one. Insertion at the head is O(1); appending at the tail or searching by value is O(n) without a tracked tail pointer.

question: Write a function to reverse a singly linked list in Dart.
answer: Walk the list once, keeping track of the previous node; for each node, redirect its next pointer to the previous node before moving forward—reversing all links in a single O(n) pass with O(1) extra space.

question: Implement a Stack (LIFO) data structure in Dart.
answer: A stack can be implemented directly on top of a Dart List, using add() for push and removeLast() for pop—both O(1) operations since they only touch the end of the underlying array.

question: Implement a Queue (FIFO) using two Stacks.
answer: Push new elements onto an 'in' stack. To dequeue, if the 'out' stack is empty, pop everything from 'in' and push it onto 'out' (reversing the order), then pop from 'out'. Each element moves between stacks at most once, giving amortized O(1) per operation overall.
tip: A classic whiteboard question testing amortized complexity reasoning, not just syntax.

question: Implement insertion and search for a Binary Search Tree in Dart.
answer: Each node holds a value and left/right child references. Insertion recursively compares the new value to the current node, going left if smaller and right if larger, until it finds an empty spot. Search follows the same comparison logic. Both run in O(log n) on a balanced tree, but degrade to O(n) on a skewed one.
tip: Mention the worst case explicitly—a BST built from already-sorted input degenerates into a linked list with O(n) operations, which is why self-balancing trees (AVL/Red-Black) exist.

question: Write a function to detect if a list contains any duplicate elements.
answer: Insert each element into a Set while iterating; if an element is already in the set, a duplicate exists. This runs in O(n) time versus O(n²) for comparing every pair.

question: Implement Kadane's algorithm to find the maximum sum of a contiguous subarray.
answer: Track a running sum that resets to the current element whenever including previous elements would make it smaller than starting fresh, and track the best sum seen so far. This solves the problem in a single O(n) pass, versus O(n²) for checking every subarray.

question: Write a function to check if a string has balanced parentheses/brackets.
answer: Push every opening bracket onto a stack; on a closing bracket, check that it matches the type on top of the stack (pop it if so, otherwise the string is unbalanced). At the end, the stack must be empty for the string to be fully balanced.
tip: Don't forget to check the stack is empty at the end—a string like '(()' passes every individual character check but has an unclosed bracket.

question: Write a function to check if two strings are anagrams of each other.
answer: Two strings are anagrams if they contain exactly the same characters with the same frequencies. Sort both strings' characters and compare (O(n log n)), or count character frequencies with a Map and compare counts (O(n), faster for longer strings).
tip: Mention both approaches and their complexity trade-off—shows you can pick the right tool, not just one memorized solution.

question: Write FizzBuzz in Dart (print Fizz/Buzz/FizzBuzz for multiples of 3/5/both from 1 to n).
answer: Check divisibility by 15 (3 and 5) first, since checking 3 and 5 separately in the wrong order would print 'Fizz' or 'Buzz' instead of 'FizzBuzz' for multiples of both.
tip: A trivial problem, but interviewers watch for the divisible-by-15-first ordering mistake as a basic sanity/attention-to-detail check.

question: Given a list containing n distinct numbers from 0 to n, find the missing one.
answer: Compute the expected sum of 0..n using the formula n*(n+1)/2, then subtract the actual sum of the given list—the difference is the missing number. This runs in O(n) time and O(1) space, avoiding a sort or a hash set.

question: Merge two already-sorted lists into one sorted list.
answer: Use two pointers, one per list, repeatedly picking the smaller of the two current elements and advancing that pointer, then append whatever remains from the list that isn't exhausted yet. This is the same merge step used inside merge sort, running in O(n + m) time.

question: Write a function to deep clone a nested Map/List structure of primitives.
answer: Recursively check each value's runtime type: if it's a Map, clone each entry recursively into a new map; if it's a List, clone each element recursively into a new list; otherwise (a primitive), copy it directly since primitives are already immutable.

question: Write a function that deeply compares two nested Lists/Maps for equality.
answer: Dart's default == on List/Map is identity-based (or only shallow via ListEquality/MapEquality from package:collection), so a custom deep check must recursively compare each nested structure, handling type mismatches, differing lengths/key sets, and primitive comparisons as base cases.
tip: Mention package:collection's DeepCollectionEquality first—reinventing this from scratch is rarely necessary in production code, but shows you understand what it does under the hood.

question: Simulate a producer-consumer pattern using a Dart Stream.
answer: A StreamController acts as the shared buffer: a 'producer' calls controller.sink.add(item) as items become available, and a 'consumer' calls controller.stream.listen((item) => process(item))—Dart's event loop naturally serializes delivery, so no manual locking is needed like in a multi-threaded language.

question: Implement a simple type-safe event bus (pub/sub) using Streams.
answer: A broadcast StreamController lets multiple independent listeners subscribe to the same event stream. Publishing calls add(event); subscribing filters by event type using stream.where((e) => e is T)—giving a decoupled way for distant parts of the app to communicate without direct references to each other.
tip: An event bus is convenient but can make data flow hard to trace in a large app—mention that Bloc/Riverpod are usually a better-structured alternative for anything beyond simple cross-cutting notifications.

question: Implement a reactive counter using GetX.
answer: GetX's .obs extension turns a value into an observable (Rx) type; wrapping UI in Obx(() => ...) automatically rebuilds whenever any observable it reads changes—no BuildContext, Provider, or StreamBuilder boilerplate required.

question: Implement state restoration so a form survives the app being killed by the OS.
answer: Mix in RestorationMixin, provide a unique restorationId, and register each piece of state that must survive process death (e.g. a RestorableTextEditingController) inside restoreState using registerForRestoration—the OS-provided restoration bucket persists these values so Android/iOS can restore the exact UI after the app is killed in the background and relaunched.
tip: This is different from simply persisting to shared_preferences—it specifically restores the in-progress UI state after an OS-initiated kill, matching native platform expectations.

question: Write a function that counts the frequency of each word in a string.
answer: Split the string on whitespace, normalize case, and use a Map<String, int> with update(word, (v) => v + 1, ifAbsent: () => 1) to tally occurrences in a single O(n) pass.

question: Write a function that splits a list into chunks of a given size.
answer: Iterate in steps of size, slicing out a sublist at each step using sublist(start, end), clamping the end index to the list's length for the final (possibly shorter) chunk. Runs in O(n) overall.
tip: A practical real use case: splitting a large list of items into pages for a paginated UI, or batching API requests to stay under a payload size limit.

question: Write a function to find the first non-repeating character in a string.
answer: Make one pass to build a character-frequency Map, then a second pass over the original string (to preserve order) returning the first character whose count is exactly 1. This runs in O(n) time with two linear passes rather than an O(n²) nested-loop comparison.
tip: Iterating the original string again (not the map, which has no guaranteed order relative to first occurrence) is what preserves correct left-to-right order.
